author: @philpalfrey3349
text:
Robert
Came across your channel a few months ago and watched every video since, really enjoying it. 
Can I please ask what is probably a stupid question. 
I have been on a recent road trip in Europe and gazing out the car window at all the wind turbines. Question:- Why do they all turn clockwise? I have tried researching it but I’m not convinced by any of the info I have read on the internet.

author: @ElizabethGreene
text: Here's a nifty thing.  The magnetic gearbox is designed to increase the speed of the generator, but you can use a similar technique for your serpentine generator coils.  If you put serpentine coils in the outer part of the gearbox instead of the magnets, the coils would "see" the rotor turning twice as fast as if you just put the coils next to the rotor.

author: @adymode
text: Careful its dangerous, those blades are heavy and can go supersonic if not damped.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: are you being serious? or is that just a joke?

replies.author: @adymode
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering No joke Maestro. Both my portable wind turbines could go supersonic when they caught a gust with more energy than the torque of their generator was able to extract (around 100watts). Drag becomes the limiting factor so momentum can build up in the blades until the tips go supersonic (like cracking whips) Both were slightly smaller than your prototype here, they would scream like an aircraft engine and go so energetic, first instinct is to run away. Quite a spectacle but do take care.

author: @whatelseison8970
text: All the experiments I've done with propellers used as wind turbines seem to indicate that you actually would get more out of this if you put your blades on facing the other way -- with the concave side facing the wind.  Just my $0.02.  I really do like the simplicity of this build though, especially the idea of using a standard broom handle. Nice job Rob, as always.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it depends what you want mate - with the convex facing the wind you get lift with the concave facing the wind you get drag

author: @mikejones-vd3fg
text: Thats really cool, I like how you found a simple way to make the blades, then im reminded of a cylinder i 3d printed and figure I could just cut that into shape as well, for those who cant get pipe this could be an alternative, just print one! I had some good luck printing off a cylinder about the same diameter in vase mode maxing out the .4mm flow and layer height to give the thickest possible layers and it came out good, flimsy but expected at that thicknes, strong enough not to fall apart and definatelye strong  enough to brace air(i think), and the weight/ material saving could double your generation with the same setup? Only issues is size but you could make  scaled down version to accomade the smaller blades or add more blades in parallel for more torque if that even works?  This is something Im deftainely  considering trying for smallscale DIY power genreation, charge a power bank etc.  The portabiliy of it makes it that much cooler, well done.

replies.text: i am glad you like it mate and that is a lot of ideas to play with - i am hoping you try a few and share your results

author: @davidblyth5495
text:
When people ask why one enjoys engineering they don't understand the joy of extracting principles from physics, applying them to a design and proving it!
Admittedly I've also been disappointed with results but that's the difference between design and development!
Well done Robert - such a joy to see such success!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: every now and then i think i should do more videos on the things that don't work - just so folks understand that better lol

replies.author: @davidblyth5495
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkeringyes, a video of "What didn't work!".
Maths and theory are only part of the journey. Then it's making mistakes.
Reality!
It could encourage people to persist!

At one stage during the beginning of my design/development career I wondered whether I should rather be digging ditches.
A week later I produced a prototype using theory I'd read up - I presented a paper on it at a conference!

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: The one thing that has always had me thinking is the curvature of the propeller blades. Yes I get it it, the curved side to the front is suposed to be the best way to get the maximum amount of wind thrust when connected to a motor as used in an airplane. But then surely in reverse mode as in a generator in order get the maximum revs / torque efficiency the hollow side of the blades would the best side for the wind to be blown onto the blades. ie full reverseability. Am I crazy to be thinking this way? Has anyone tried the reverse way? Does it make any difference?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it depends what you want mate - if you reverse them you you get a drag effect if you leave them as is you get lift

replies.author: @bartronicsecurity
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Hmm. Not sure I agree fully. Yes more lift as used in an airplane but I think also more turning force if the wind blows into the curved side meaning more efficiently ultimately.  I'm going to try that one day when I'm back to playing with my wind stuff.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @bartronicsecurity well - doesn't sound right to me - but what they hey it's only an idea i have - cheers

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering If you have a member of your channel who has simulation software then this would be one way to test the idea.

text: Why 8mm bar as opposed to bolts of the same diameter?

replies.author: @OldManSparkplug
replies.text: Makes perfect sense, I've got some bolts in the junk bin that look about the right size, I'll let you know once the print is done.  Using PETG for the filament, PLA's probably not going to take the strain long term.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @OldManSparkplug cool - to strengthen PLA i use a surface coating of thin cyanoacrylic glue - you wouldn't believe how strong that is lol

replies.author: @OldManSparkplug
replies.text: I've done the same, but I usually do a light dusting of baking soda first and use as low viscosity cyanoacrylate as possible.  Capillary action pulls it into the layer lines and the soda cures it almost instantly, it does look bad but sands brilliantly.

author: @SustainableSailing
text:
Brilliant.
Only concern is that to build so that it can last real use on a boat (for example) in a salt water environment will immediately increase the cost (using 316 stainless steel being one example).
The Rutland is supposed to be last years when left out in all weather. For 10% of the cost to be cheaper it needs to last more than 10% as long.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: make it out of plastic in total

replies.author: @SustainableSailing
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I get very confused by all the choices of plastic 🤔
So plastic axle and bearings? Presumably if the parts of the generator are coated in resin they would have a good life despite the environment.
Although I'm still looking forward to seeing if these small Darwin collections with turbine might be practical.
One of the things I don't yet understand how to handle is our battery bank is 48volt. I like the ease of your serpentine generators but guess I would need a mighty mite?

author: @Dalorath
text:
Remember when i suggested Cylinders in consecutive rings to see if it would spin, i think i had the right idea, but the cylinders needs a bit of a twist... It would show the air where its meant to go. What  is the max friction of wind between 2 surfaces? I think a spiraled cylindrical conical acceptance face, would allow air in and not birds... If given a twist, it would allow for mechanical motion, but im not sure if it can be efficient or not, since its neer been tested yet. At least not what i have in mind, im sure fins are cool but im thinking of some cool spiral channels like tesla disks for his turbine, but spiraled to accept wind or water depending on the viscosity and distance between the plates and the depth of length of the disk spirals... Kinda like the same width of radiators on your car or air conditioner, but spiraled to direct air and cause rotation.capturing more of hte wind might simply be a matter of making the blades spiral in conical structure to a certtain depth before it looses its benefit. Too deep of fins, and it might huinder the accleration of the air or water through it.,
Sorry for the lumped paragraph, im prety drunk lol.. i cant do much lately since my ankles busted...

author: @mreyes3591
text: I've been making wind turbines out of car alternators the ones I use are from old Chevy trucks our wind speed here is between 50 to 95 mph I need a good voltage regulator so I don't over power the salt batteries?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome work mate

replies.author: @JSabh
replies.text: I use a solar charger, super simple and the varying voltage is what it is designed for. Also really cool to have the bluetooth app and track the charge amount total and output totals as well as give an awesome graphed baseline for the "heartbeat" of your generator.

replies.author: @ravenmad9225
replies.text: 50 to 95mph is very windy.

author: @Ilamarea
text: Honestly... It's a tad dumb. Just pull a pin and take the blades off. Folding makes sense when it needs to be done remotely, like on a space station.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you do know this is a hot selling product right?

replies.author: @Ilamarea
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering At one point a "pet rock" was a hot selling product. But then again, I think our cars, even the electric ones, are extremely dumb as well. We should be riding in bolids a third the size and a quarter the weight with small trailers for extra passengers, cargo and batteries when needed instead of this insane, nonsensical waste.

Humanity's problem is that we just can't escape our programming. Cars are so dumb because that's what we learned cars are when we were kids.

replies.author: @edwardphillips8460
replies.text: Can’t wait to see your designs.

replies.author: @AndreaDingbatt
replies.text:
​​@Ilamarea suspect that Robert was just trying to prove a point,,,,in that when we buy a Pre-Made Portable Generator,,,,
We are being Ripped Off by the Companies that make and sell them....

Without saying That too Obviously!!

Of course I could be Wrong.....

By the way, about my pet Rock,,,,,
I have noticed that it has a tendency to "Go Off"  its Food every now and then.....

Any Tips for getting it to Eat More?!

replies.author: @skampp
replies.text: @Ilamarea Not a bad idea Ilamarea.  But rather than say how dumb humanity is, how about design a car that's 1/3 the size and 1/4 the weight, make it run, and put it up on a youtube channel for dumb humanity to learn from?  That's how we all get better rather than being divisive for no good reason.

author: @blitzsuccess2303
text: What do I need to print or make this ? Lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: some soil pipe and the files in the description

replies.author: @blitzsuccess2303
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I mean what do I to with the files - sorry! What kind of Mc I need for the files

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @blitzsuccess2303 o ok - sorry mate i didn't get that - the files are exporable in obj, stl, glv or svg - so they can be imported into a slicing program like CURA of a cad program like fusion 360 then either printed or modified - you can send the files to a print service who would print them for you if you don't have a printer - or you could replicate the parts in wood or plastic with hand tools
